concept: Falls Short / Less Than / Does Not Reach
description: The verb of magnitude comparison announcing that one quantity does not reach another in amount. It states a quantitative relation without lexically saying that the smaller quantity is inadequate or worse. Context still determines the relevant measure, whether the quantities are comparable, and whether the comparison is being used responsibly. A comparison may state both quantities or leave a reference threshold recoverable from context.
sound_symbolism: The word offers a mnemonic image of a gap. 'The' begins with a sustained dental fricative, and 'noi' closes the comparison with a distinct contour. The association can help recall the direction of the relation; it does not make a comparison honest, mindful, or free of value judgment.
grammatical_notes: With two explicit quantitative arguments, the evaluated quantity comes first and mandatory 'nela' marks the boundary before the reference: '[quantity 1] nela [quantity 2] thenoi'. Example: 'ta shao nela ta phoi thenoi.' (Three falls short of nine / 3 < 9.) The reference may be omitted only when context supplies it, as in falling short of an already named criterion. Can be negated with 'ma' to express 'does not fall short' (greater than or equal). Real-world uses should name comparable quantities and state the measure or criterion when it is not obvious. Can be nominalized to discuss falling short or deficit. Questions use 'wa': 'wa wi shao nela ta shao thenoi.' (Does six fall short of three? / Is 6 < 3?)

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Separating a magnitude relation from an evaluation can support observation without automatically labeling the smaller quantity deficient. Three is not necessarily inadequate because it does not reach nine. The lexical distinction does not prevent comparison from being used harmfully.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: 'Thenoi' supports communication that distinguishes a factual magnitude claim from a claim of inadequacy. Speakers must still examine the selected measure, the purpose of comparison, and its effects.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Practical comparisons such as one plot yielding less grain than another can remain descriptive rather than evaluative. 'Thenoi' supports that distinction without claiming that traditional measurement was uniformly free of hierarchy or judgment.

tags.cognition: Announces magnitude relationship requiring mental comparison
tags.spatial: Frames relative position in numerical space—one quantity does not extend to another
